metadata.summary: Eliminate static credentials via cloud identity (IRSA, Workload Identity, OIDC) and short-lived dynamic secrets from a backend; use ESO as the Kubernetes bridge.

content.title: Secrets Management
content.summary: Secrets management in 2025-2026 means eliminating static, long-lived credentials: apps authenticate via cloud identity (IRSA, Workload Identity, OIDC) and receive short-lived dynamic credentials from a secrets backend. Kubernetes integration uses External Secrets Operator (ESO) as the standard bridge between any backend and pod-level secret injection. Rotation must be automated on a 30-day or shorter cycle.

why.p: Static secrets stored in environment variables or Kubernetes native Secrets (base64, not encrypted at rest by default) are the most common breach vector. Dynamic secrets from Vault's database engine issue credentials that expire in minutes — a compromised secret becomes useless before an attacker can act. OIDC-based auth for CI/CD (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) eliminates long-lived service account keys entirely.

list.item: Application needs database credentials, API keys, or TLS certificates at runtime
Kubernetes workloads require secrets without storing them as native K8s Secrets in plain base64
CI/CD pipelines must authenticate to cloud providers without stored access keys
Compliance requires secret rotation, audit logging, and access control (SOC2, HIPAA, PCI-DSS)
Development-only local environment — .env files are acceptable; do not add Vault overhead
Secrets that are truly public (public API keys, non-sensitive config values) — use ConfigMaps, not secrets backends
Single-cloud project where the cloud provider's native secrets service (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager) covers all use cases — adding Vault is unnecessary operational overhead

rule.statement: Every secret must have an owner, a rotation schedule, and an audit log — secrets without these three properties are unmanaged liabilities.
rule.rationale: Unrotated secrets that are breached remain valid indefinitely. Without an audit log, breach investigation cannot determine when and which secrets were accessed.

list.item: Creation: generate with sufficient entropy (min 128-bit); never use predictable values
Storage: encrypted at rest in a secrets backend; never in code, .env files, or ConfigMaps
Access: RBAC with audit logging; each service gets its own identity, not shared credentials
Rotation: automated 30-day max cycle; applications must handle rotation without restart
Revocation: immediate revocation capability; test the revocation path before production

rule.statement: Prefer dynamic secrets (Vault database engine, cloud IAM short-lived tokens) over static secrets wherever the target system supports it.
rule.rationale: Dynamic secrets are issued per-request with a short TTL (minutes to hours). A compromised dynamic credential expires before an attacker can typically exploit it. Static secrets are valid indefinitely until manually rotated.

example.note: Vault database secrets engine: application requests PostgreSQL credentials, Vault creates a role with 15-minute TTL, credentials are valid only for that request lifecycle. On expiry, the role is automatically revoked.
Shared DATABASE_URL in .env file with a password that was last rotated 18 months ago and is used by 5 different services.

rule.statement: Use HashiCorp Vault for multi-cloud or hybrid environments; use the cloud provider's native secrets service (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault) for single-cloud workloads.
rule.rationale: Vault adds operational overhead (HA cluster, unseal management, upgrades). If all workloads are in AWS, the operational simplicity of AWS Secrets Manager outweighs Vault's cross-cloud capabilities.

list.item: HashiCorp Vault: multi-cloud, dynamic secrets, complex PKI, regulated industries
AWS Secrets Manager: AWS-native, managed rotation for RDS/Redshift, pay-per-secret
External Secrets Operator: Kubernetes bridge — syncs any backend into K8s Secrets
SOPS: GitOps workflows, encrypts files in repos, no runtime server required

rule.statement: Use OIDC-based authentication for CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions id-token, GitLab CI JWT) to eliminate long-lived service account keys entirely.
rule.rationale: Service account keys stored in CI secrets rotate infrequently and are accessible to all pipeline users. OIDC tokens are issued per-run, expire in 15 minutes, and are scoped to the specific repository and branch.

antipattern.description: Storing cloud provider access keys (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, GCP_SA_KEY) as CI/CD secrets.
antipattern.reason: Long-lived keys exposed in logs or to pipeline contributors grant persistent access. OIDC eliminates this risk entirely with no key to steal.

rule.statement: Use External Secrets Operator (ESO) as the standard Kubernetes integration layer — it supports all major backends (AWS SM, GCP SM, Azure KV, Vault) with a unified API.
rule.rationale: Native Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted at rest by default. ESO syncs from an encrypted backend and recreates the K8s Secret automatically on rotation, without restarting pods.

example.code: # ClusterSecretStore — backend connection apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1 kind: ClusterSecretStore metadata: name: aws-secrets-manager spec: provider: aws: service: SecretsManager region: us-east-1 auth: jwt: serviceAccountRef: name: external-secrets-sa namespace: external-secrets --- # ExternalSecret — specific secret sync apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1 kind: ExternalSecret metadata: name: db-credentials namespace: production spec: refreshInterval: 1h secretStoreRef: name: aws-secrets-manager kind: ClusterSecretStore target: name: db-credentials creationPolicy: Owner data: - secretKey: password remoteRef: key: prod/app/db property: password

rule.statement: On AWS EKS, use IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) to bind a Kubernetes ServiceAccount to an AWS IAM role — no AWS credentials in pods or Secrets.
rule.rationale: IRSA uses OIDC token exchange: the pod presents a K8s JWT, AWS STS exchanges it for temporary credentials scoped to the IAM role. Credentials auto-rotate every hour.

example.code: apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: app-sa namespace: production annotations: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/app-prod-role
example.note: The IAM role trust policy must allow sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity from the OIDC provider and restrict the sub claim to the specific namespace:serviceaccount.

section.name: security-hardening

rule.statement: Enable envelope encryption for etcd using a KMS provider key — base64 encoding in native Secrets is not encryption.
rule.rationale: Without envelope encryption, anyone with etcd access (backup files, etcd snapshots) can read all Kubernetes Secrets in plaintext after a simple base64 decode.

rule.statement: Never log secrets: ensure application code masks credential values in logs and that Kubernetes audit logs do not capture Secret data.
rule.rationale: Secrets in logs are effectively unencrypted and distributed — logs go to ELK/Loki, S3, and potentially third-party log aggregators.

antipattern.description: Storing secrets in ConfigMaps because they are easier to manage.
antipattern.reason: ConfigMaps have no access control differentiation from regular config. Any pod that can read ConfigMaps in a namespace can read all secrets stored there.

section.reference: Vault policy template with least-privilege path scoping

template.purpose: ESO ClusterSecretStore + ExternalSecret for AWS Secrets Manager
Vault policy template with least-privilege path scoping
LLM prompt for auditing secrets posture
